all_seals.find_period: Late Akkadian / early Ur III (ca. 2200-2113 BC)
all_seals.inscription_translation: Shu-ilishu, interpreter of the Meluhha language
all_seals.notes: The single most famous direct cross-cultural artefact: an Akkadian-style cylinder seal explicitly identifying its owner as a Meluhha-language interpreter. Its presence implies bilingualism existed in the Sargonic-period Mesopotamian administration.
One of the Akkadian-context Indus seals from Ur excavated by Woolley. Catalogued by Gadd 1932. Frenez 2018 lists this in the Mesopotamia-found Indus seal corpus.
all_seals.signs_source: Gadd 1932 (Iraq); Frenez 2018 plate IV | Laursen 2010 Table 1 row 16 (Gadd 1932: pl. I, no. 2, site=Ur)
all_seals.phase24_crossref_rationale: Gadd 1932 pl. I no. 2 (round Ur seal with zebu motif). Best-fit Laursen Table 1 row for the BM 122187 entry.

all_seals.notes: First of the so-called 'Persian Gulf' / Gulf-Type seals identified by Gadd 1932. Round shape, distinct from the square Indus seals. Found in Mesopotamian context but with Indus-derived inscription.
all_seals.signs_source: Gadd 1932 #1; Laursen 2010 PCA classification | Laursen 2010 Table 1 row 17 (Gadd 1932: pl. I, no. 3, site=Ur)
all_seals.phase24_crossref_rationale: Gadd 1932 pl. I no. 3 (square Ur seal).

all_seals.notes: Companion seal to GADD_1, same find context.
all_seals.signs_source: Gadd 1932 #2; Laursen 2010 | Laursen 2010 Table 1 row 18 (Gadd 1932: pl. I, no. 4, site=Ur)

all_seals.notes: Cited by Mallowan 1947 (Iraq IX) and standard Indus-Mesopotamia syntheses.
One of two Indus seals from Kish in the Ashmolean.
Cited by Wheeler and Possehl. Susa was a major contact-zone city between Mesopotamia and the eastern trade.
all_seals.signs_source: Wheeler 1968; Possehl 2002 | Laursen 2010 Table 1 row 14 (Amiet 1972: no. 1643, site=Susa)
all_seals.phase24_crossref_rationale: Amiet 1972 no. 1643 — Susa Gulf INDUS seal (Laursen Table 1 row 14).

all_seals.notes: The reverse case: a Gulf-Type seal found at the Indus port of Lothal. Reported by S. R. Rao 1963 (Antiquity 37:96-99). First clear archaeological evidence of Persian Gulf -> Indus trade contact.

all_seals.inscription_reading: (unread; carries Indus signs in distinctive 'twins'-prefix Gulf style)
all_seals.notes: From the Tell F6 'Palace' deposit; one of ~24 Dilmun-Type seals from Failaka with Indus signs. Featured on the cover of David-Cuny & Neyme 2016.

Akkadian cylinder seal with Indus markhor (mountain goat) iconography, indicating a Meluhhan trader carrier. One of the ~18 ANE cylinder seals identified by Kalyanaraman as having Meluhhan iconography.

all_seals.find_spot: Konar Sandal South, Halil Rud / Jiroft, Iran
all_seals.inscription_reading: (no inscription; carries Indus animal-head whirl motif)
all_seals.notes: Local-style adaptation of the Indus 'multi-headed creature' template. Demonstrates Indus-Marhashi cultural interaction. Vidale & Frenez 2015.

all_seals.inscription_reading: (short Indus sign sequence + South-East Iranian deity scene)
all_seals.notes: Combines Halil Rud religious iconography with Indus-script signs of the 'Persian Gulf round stamp seal' family. Direct evidence of Indus-Marhashi-Dilmun trader interaction.
all_seals.source: Vidale, Desset & Frenez 2021 (Journal of Archaeological Studies); Ascalone 2008

all_seals.inscription_reading: Yagli'el, servant of the goddess Panipa
all_seals.notes: JUST PUBLISHED Apr 2026 (Laursen, Al-Shaikh, Reeler, Lombard, Marchesi - JNES). Tetrahedron shape and ~3cm size match the Kanmer (Gujarat, India) Indus identity-tokens almost exactly. First clear evidence of Indus *administrative-token* technology being adopted in Dilmun under named royal authority. Naming King Yagli'el ties this to the dynastic chronology of Bronze Age Dilmun.
all_seals.source: Laursen, Al-Shaikh, Reeler, Lombard & Marchesi 2026 (JNES); summary at gdnonline.com 2026-04-19

inscribed_seals.signs_source: Asko Parpola personal communication, in Laursen 2010 footnote 2; sign IDs from Parpola 1994b: 70-78, fig. 5.1
parpola_attestation_notes: Sequence 16-145 also at beginning of Kalibangan K-69 to K-75 (Joshi & Parpola 1987:312-313)
Signs 16 and 364 co-occur on Mohenjo-Daro M-798 unicorn seal (Shah & Parpola 1991:68)

47.source: Parpola 2010 (the central case study); Parpola 1994a Ch.10

60.source: Laursen 2010 fn 2 cites this as 'badly drawn fish'; Parpola family

145.source: Parpola 1994a fig. 5.1; co-occurs with numerical signs to form astral names

147.source: Parpola 1994b sign list; Laursen 2010 fn 2 (Janabiyah seal pos. 1b)

311.source: Parpola 2010 ('fig + fish' compound); 'V' sign per Parpola fig. 5.1 no. 311

16.gloss: rare in Indus Valley, common in Near East
16.source: Laursen 2010 fn 2 attests as Janabiyah seal pos. 5; sequence 16-145 at start of Kalibangan K-69 to K-75
16.noted_pattern: 16-145 sequence repeats in Kalibangan K-69 to K-75 (Joshi & Parpola 1987)

364.source: Laursen 2010 fn 2 attests as Janabiyah seal pos. 2; co-occurs with 16 on Mohenjo-Daro M-798 unicorn seal (Shah & Parpola 1991:68)

126.source: Laursen 2010 fn 2 attests as Janabiyah seal pos. 4; alternates 125 and 128
126.uncertainty_note: Parpola flagged 'very uncertain' even in his attested reading

53.source: Laursen 2010 fn 2 attests as Janabiyah seal pos. 1a (alt: 60 'fish')

1.gloss: man / human; rebus aaL = 'man, person, slave' (Dravidian)
1.source: Parpola 1994a ch.13; iconic 'man with raised arm'; Tamil aaL = man, vir = hero
1.iconic: stick figure with one or both arms raised

99.gloss: bow; rebus vil = 'bow' (Tamil); also vil-vaa-LL = bowman/archer in Sangam Tamil
99.source: Parpola 1994a fig. 5.1; iconic 'bow' or 'man with bow'
99.iconic: bow shape, sometimes with archer holding it

117.gloss: wheel / chakra; rebus ar = Tamil 'spoke (of wheel) / file (tool)'
117.source: Mahadevan 1977; Parpola 1994a; iconic six- or eight-spoked wheel

124.gloss: pot/jar; rebus kuTam = 'pot' in all Dravidian languages
124.source: Parpola 2010 sec. 'iconic shape...' + M-478 tablet anchor (fig. 5)
124.iconic: V-shaped offering vessel (possibly cross-section of a pot)
124.iconographic_anchor: M-478: V-shaped sign in inscription = V-shaped offering vessel held by worshipper before sacred tree

264.source: Parpola 1994a ch.13; iconic 'goddess with horns' or generic female sign
264.iconic: horned female figure or stylized goddess

86.source: Parpola 1994a fig. 5.1; numerical sign sequence position 1

87.gloss: two vertical strokes; rebus = veL/veeL 'white/bright', muruku-veeL composite name of Murukan; veN-miin = 'white star' = Venus
87.source: Parpola 2010 sec. 'Murukan and Venus'; cf. Old Tamil veN-miin = Venus
87.homonymy: veL ('white/bright', name component of Murukan), veLi ('open space, atmosphere'), veeL ('desire')

88.gloss: three vertical strokes, possibly 'three' or 'three worlds'

91.gloss: six (numerical); aru-miin = Pleiades ('six stars') in Old Tamil
91.source: Parpola 2010 sec. 'Compounds with fish signs and Indian mythology'; central case study

92.gloss: seven (numerical); eZu-miin = Ursa Major ('seven stars') in Old Tamil
92.source: Parpola 2010 sec. 'Compounds with fish signs and Indian mythology'; H-9 seal carries '7 + fish' alone

175.gloss: spinner's spindle; rebus = katir 'to shine, be radiant' (often connected with Murukan and the rising sun); katir-k-kaTavuL = 'radiant deity' = sun
175.source: Parpola 2010 sec. 'Identifying Murukan name in the Indus texts'; M-241 + M-112 seals

261.gloss: two intersecting circles; rebus = muruku 'young man / Murukan / ear-ring / bangle'; central name of the Old Tamil war-god
261.source: Parpola 2010 sec. 'Identifying Murukan name'; defended across Parpola 1994 + 2010 + 2018
261.iconic: two intersecting circles (likely depicting paired ear-rings)
261.homonymy: muruku ('young man, Murukan'), muruku ('ring, ear-ring, bangle')
261.co_occurrence_anchor: high frequency on inscribed Harappan stoneware bangles -> 'bangle' meaning corroborated by artifact context

281.gloss: five-striped palm squirrel; rebus = piLLai 'child, infant, young of animals'; forms diminutives like Muruka-p-piLLa
281.source: Parpola 2010 sec. 'PiLLai young as attribute of squirrel and of Muruku'; co-occurs with 261 (muruku) on M-1202, H-771, Nausharo
281.iconic: squirrel with tail up, head down, four feet on a vertical tree-trunk

311_fig.gloss: banyan/fig; rebus = vaTa 'north/northern'; 'fig + fish' = vaTa-miin = north star (Old Tamil; Alcor in Ursa Major in late tradition; Thuban in 3rd millennium BCE)
311_fig.source: Parpola 2010 sec. 'Banyan fig and the pole star'
311_fig.iconic: three-branched fig tree (cf. Nausharo painted goblet c. 2600-2550 BCE)

87_veLLi_synonym.gloss: two long vertical strokes used as a SYNONYM of fish/star: veLLi = Venus + 'star' in general (Tamil)
87_veLLi_synonym.source: Parpola 2010 sec. 'Additional cross-checking'; M-172 + H-6 inscriptions show 87 and 47 in same context with same preceding+following sign
87_veLLi_synonym.compound: vaan-veLLi 'sky-star' = 'star (general)' in Tamil; viTi-veLLi = viTi-miin = 'dawn star' = Venus
87_veLLi_synonym.note: Synonymy with sign 47 (miin) is structural evidence, not just rebus-coincidence

_yoke_carrier.gloss: yoke carrier (the figure carrying a beam across shoulders); rebus kavai = 'yoke' in Tamil; cf. kavai worship
_yoke_carrier.source: Parpola 1981 'On the Harappan yoke-carrier pictogram and the kavai worship' (Proceedings 5th Tamil Studies Conference); Parpola 1994a
_yoke_carrier.iconic: human figure with horizontal beam across shoulders, jars hanging from each end
_yoke_carrier.sign_id_note: Parpola sign-list ID requires Parpola 1994a fig. 5.1 reference; not yet integer-resolved in our data

_buffalo.gloss: water buffalo; rebus erumai = 'female buffalo' (Tamil); also Parpola: yelu = 'royal buffalo'
_buffalo.source: Parpola 2004 'From archaeology to stratigraphy of Vedic syncretism: The banyan tree and the water buffalo as Harappan-Dravidian symbols of royalty'; Parpola 1985 The Sky-Garment
_buffalo.royalty_anchor: buffalo + banyan = royal symbolism inherited by Yama (Indo-Aryan god of death) -> Vedic stratigraphy supports Harappan origin

_three_strokes_worlds.gloss: three vertical strokes; per Parpola 2010 'denotes the three worlds' (intervening space, atmosphere, three lokas)
_three_strokes_worlds.source: Parpola 2010 sec. 'Additional cross-checking'; cf. Sanskrit antarikSa 'intervening space'
_three_strokes_worlds.distinct_from_sign_88: Sign 88 in our map is 'three short strokes (numeral)'; this entry refers specifically to the LONG-stroke variant Parpola identifies as 'three worlds'

_lion.gloss: lion or large feline; rebus araL/ari = 'lion' or 'master/king' (Tamil)
_lion.source: Parpola 1994a Ch. 14; cf. Mahadevan 1977; central to Phase-28 sign-allograph extension

_eagle.gloss: bird/eagle; possible rebus to 'puL' = bird or to mythical Garuda; uncertain

_yoke_carrier_v2.gloss: strengthened entry: yoke-carrier; rebus kavai = 'yoke + worship'; Parpola defended in 1981, 1994a, 2010
_yoke_carrier_v2.source: Parpola 1981 'On the Harappan yoke-carrier pictogram'; reaffirmed in Parpola 1994a Ch. 13 + Parpola 2010
_yoke_carrier_v2.iconic: human figure with horizontal beam + jars hanging
_yoke_carrier_v2.strengthening_note: Phase-28: promoted to high-confidence based on Parpola's 30-year defense across multiple papers

_buffalo_v2.gloss: strengthened entry: water buffalo; central royal-symbolism animal of Harappan iconography
_buffalo_v2.royalty_anchor: Inherited by Yama (Indo-Aryan god of death) per Parpola 2004 stratigraphy of Vedic syncretism

janabiyah_reading.summary: 3 confirmed miin (fish/star) + 1 likely miin alternate (53|60) + 3 uncertain/unread signs. The reading is dominated by the 'miin' element, suggesting a name with multiple star-or-fish references. Per Parpola's Dravidian hypothesis, names like aru-miin (Pleiades), eZu-miin (Ursa Major), vaTa-miin (north star) all use the miin morpheme. Three miin in one personal name is consistent with a high-status name carrying multiple astral epithets.
janabiyah_reading.expected_attested_form: Sumerian/Akkadian transliteration would render *miin* as syllables like 'mi-in', 'me-en', 'mi-na', 'me-na'. Search Mesopotamian PNs for: mi-in, me-en, mi-na, mi-il, me-il (Sumerian rendering of Dravidian *miin*) or mu-li (Sumerian 'star').

Sh.note: Indus trading colony — most northeasterly Harappan site

Failaka_seal.note: Not in CISI Vol 3 Part 3; seals at Kuwait National Museum

Bahrain_seal.note: Janabiyah seal #10 carries the only fully-resolved Indus-style sign sequence in the contact zone

Mesopotamia_seal.site: Mesopotamia (various: Ur, Nippur, Tell Asmar, Umma, Tello/Girsu)

phase27_overrides._doc: Phase-26d's auto-prefix-matcher mis-assigned 3 hand-encoded Phase-22 seals because their catalogue IDs use site-name conventions, not CISI prefixes. Phase-27 adds explicit overrides keyed by catalogue_id. Lookup precedence: explicit override > longest CISI prefix match > unknown.

LOTHAL_PERSIAN_GULF_SEAL.note: Found in core Indus Valley but is a Persian-Gulf-style seal

iconographic_anchors.iconic_evidence: The 'fish' sign is depicted in the mouth of a fish-eating crocodile (gharial, Gavialis gangeticus). The pictorial context proves the sign denotes 'fish' irrespective of any rebus argument.
iconographic_anchors.implication: Any phonetic-decoder hypothesis must respect sign 47 as iconographically 'fish'. The Dravidian rebus *miin* (fish/star) is consistent with this; competing readings that don't have a 'fish' meaning are falsified.

iconographic_anchors.iconic_evidence: Tablet appears to mention 'four pots of fish' as offerings. The 'fish' sign appears with a numerical sign ('four') and a pot/jar sign in an offering context.
iconographic_anchors.implication: Confirms sign 47 retains its iconic 'fish' meaning in at least some contexts (offering / commodity tablets), as opposed to its rebus meaning 'star' in seal-onomastic contexts.

iconographic_anchors.iconic_evidence: The 'V-shaped' sign in the inscription is depicted on the right end of the same tablet as a V-shaped offering vessel held by a kneeling worshipper before a sacred tree.
iconographic_anchors.implication: The intended and iconic meanings of the V-shaped sign coincide ('pot/offering vessel'). Independent confirmation of one Dravidian rebus reading: kuTam = 'pot'.

iconographic_anchors.iconic_reading: Pleiades / Ursa Major (eZu-miin = 'seven stars' = Ursa Major)
iconographic_anchors.iconic_evidence: The entire inscription on this large seal is the two-sign sequence 'seven vertical strokes' + 'fish'. Big dedicatory seals in Mesopotamia were sometimes presented to divinities; in Old Tamil ezhu-miin = Ursa Major.
iconographic_anchors.implication: Single-sign-pair seal whose context (large dedicatory seal) and content (numerical + fish) jointly support the Dravidian astral reading. Strong typological anchor.

iconographic_anchors.iconic_reading: vaTa-miin = north star (Old Tamil; original referent: pole star Thuban in 3rd millennium BCE)
iconographic_anchors.iconic_evidence: Recurring 'fig + fish' sign sequence. The Vedic and Hindu texts repeatedly refer to a heavenly fig tree (cosmic banyan held up in the sky by VaruNa).
iconographic_anchors.implication: Dravidian compound vaTa-miin = 'banyan-star' + 'rope-star' + 'north-star' (homonymy). Three semantic threads converge on the pole star; the Indus 'fig + fish' compound matches.

iconographic_anchors.iconic_evidence: Anthropomorphic deity inside a fig tree, flanked at the bottom by a star on either side.
iconographic_anchors.implication: Iconographic confirmation of fig+star (= banyan + Pleiades / north star) co-occurrence in Harappan religion. Supports vaTa-miin reading.

iconographic_anchors.iconic_evidence: Faience tablet whose obverse carries the 'two intersecting circles' sign sequence and whose reverse depicts an anthropomorphic deity sitting on a throne, surrounded by a kneeling worshipper and a snake on either side. Murukan in South Indian tradition is associated with snake cult.
iconographic_anchors.implication: Iconographic context corroborates muruku = name of a deity (Murukan / war-god). Tightens phoneme map entry for sign 261.

iconographic_anchors.iconic_reading: muruku + fig (Murukan-piLLai associated with the Seven Mothers / Pleiades, his nurses)
iconographic_anchors.iconic_evidence: Famous 'fig deity' seal. The deity standing inside the fig tree wears bangles on both arms. Seven anthropomorphic figures at the bottom (in traditional female hairstyle) likely represent the 'Seven Mothers' = Pleiades, the child-granting and child-killing goddesses.
iconographic_anchors.implication: Triangulates muruku/Murukan + Pleiades + bangle iconography on a single seal. The Vedic / South Indian tradition that Murukan's mothers/nurses are the Pleiades (krittikah) appears already in Harappan art.

iconographic_anchors.iconic_reading: muruku-piLLai (= 'young Murukan' / 'son of Murukan' or Muruka-p-piLLa, an attested Tamil name)
iconographic_anchors.iconic_evidence: Recurring sign sequence: 'two intersecting circles' (muruku, Murukan) + 'squirrel' (piLLai, 'young one / child / son'). The compound matches an actually attested Tamil compound for affectionate diminutives (e.g. Jaffna Tamil Muruka-p-piLLa).
iconographic_anchors.implication: Two-sign Indus compound has a precise lexical Tamil match. Independent corroboration of both phoneme readings (muruku for 261 + piLLai for 281).

iconographic_anchors.iconic_evidence: Same 'intersecting circles' + 'squirrel' compound as M-1202 but on a moulded tablet from Harappa. Independent attestation at a separate Indus site.
iconographic_anchors.implication: Cross-site replication of the 261+281 compound -> the muruku-piLLai reading is not site-specific.

iconographic_anchors.iconic_evidence: Detailed engraving of a five-striped palm squirrel hanging head-down on a tree-trunk, in the typical pose used by squirrels to absorb sun on a vertical trunk.
iconographic_anchors.implication: Iconographic confirmation that sign 281 depicts the five-striped palm squirrel specifically. The aNil (squirrel) reading and the rebus piLLai (= 'child') both follow.

iconographic_anchors.iconic_reading: veL/veeL (white/bright, name component of Murukan; veN-miin = Venus)
iconographic_anchors.iconic_evidence: Recurring co-occurrence of 'two long vertical strokes' with 'fish' (87+47 = veN-miin = white star = Venus, the brightest morning/evening star).
iconographic_anchors.implication: If 87 is veL/veeL, then 87+47 yields the actually-attested Old Tamil compound veN-miin (= Venus). Independent cross-check between sign 87 and sign 47 readings.

M126.iconic: rare in Indus, common in Near East

M016.iconic: Janabiyah seal pos 5; rare in Indus, common in Near East

fish._doc: Per Parpola 1994a Ch. 10 + Parpola 2010, all signs depicting a fish (plain, with roof, with fins, with trefoil, with horizontal lines, etc.) are interpreted as the same Dravidian rebus *miin* (fish/star). Signs 50, 52, 145, 147 add diacritical modifiers (likely vowel/consonant qualifiers in the underlying compound name).

numerals._doc: Parpola 1994a fig. 5.1 sequence 86-95 are the numerical signs. Sign 86 = 1, 87 = 2, ... 95 = 10. Compounds with fish (47) yield aru-miin (Pleiades, 6+fish), eZu-miin (Ursa Major, 7+fish), vaTa-miin (north star, fig+fish).

intersecting_circles.iconic: two intersecting circles (with various stroke variants)
intersecting_circles._doc: Parpola 2010 reads as muruku ('young man / Murukan / ear-ring / bangle'). The variant signs 262, 263 are likely allographs with minor stroke differences.

fig_tree._doc: Parpola 2010: the 'fig + fish' compound = vaTa-miin = north star. Sign 311 in Parpola 1994b numbering.
